**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I dares to say that I have not heard from you in quite some time. Five years, to be precise. It is a cruel thing, I must admit, to be so far removed from the ones I love most. Yet here I am, in the middle of a storm, with the sea roaring and the wind howling, and the lighthouse beam slicing through the darkness like a blade of light in the night. I have not slept since the gales have been howling, and I am not sure if I shall see another dawn. 

I remember the day you left, with your mother and I, to take up your education in London. You were a bright young lady, full of promise and potential, and I was proud to call you my daughter. But the world is a cruel place, and I fear that the path you chose may have been one of sorrow. I have heard of your marriage, and the news was like a dagger to the heart. I did not know how to write, and I did not know how to speak, for days.

**My Dearest Daughter,**  

**I Write This Letter in the Tremulous Light of a Lighthouse,**  
**Where the Wind Howls as if the Very Sky Were Dying,**  
**And the Sea Howls in Response,**  
**As If the Ocean Were in Agony of Its Own Existence.**  

**You Are Not the Only One Who Has Been Silenced by Time,**  
**But You, My Dearest Daughter,**

My Dearest Eleanor, 

I write to you now, with a heavy heart, for I have not penned a word to you in nigh on five years. The sea, it seems, has been a cruel master, and I find myself in a most precarious position, with the tempest of the night to come. I pray to the heavens that you may not read these words, for I have not the strength to endure the storm, and I fear for your sake. 

I have not seen you since the day you left, and I have not the heart to write, but I have the strength to endure.
